Case study 01 · 2024 · Academic project

Fundación Sí

Volunteers were managing night patrols through WhatsApp group chats — no structure, no history, no reliable way to share updates in real time. The goal was to centralize everything into one tool that worked in low-light, high-stress conditions — without adding complexity.

Role
UX/UI Designer
Type
Mobile app · End-to-end
Methods
Research · Double Diamond
Tools
Figma
Overview
Context

Fundación Sí conducts night patrols to assist homeless people. In 2024, this population increased by 14% in Barcelona. Coordination happened entirely via WhatsApp groups.

Problem

During patrols, volunteers had no reliable way to access or update information in real time. Updates got lost in chat threads. Coordination was slow. The context — nighttime, outdoors, under pressure — made every extra step costly.

Solution

A mobile app that centralizes patrol coordination — routes, reports, contacts and updates — in one place. Designed specifically for low-light, high-stress field conditions.

Design process
01
Discover
Interviews with volunteers — tools used, pain points, context of use during actual night patrols.
02
Define
User persona, HMW questions, clear problem definition: real-time info access in low-light, high-stress conditions.
03
Develop
Crazy 8 workshop, sitemap, wireframes and prototype tested with real users. Iterated based on feedback.
04
Deliver
High-fidelity UI designed for night use: Roboto for legibility, minimal cognitive load, 3-tab navigation.
The app

Designed for the nighttime context

The home screen gives volunteers everything at a glance — stats that motivate, upcoming tours by location, and fast access to friends' reports. No unnecessary steps, no social media switching.

The purple palette and Roboto typeface were chosen for legibility in low-light outdoor conditions, where volunteers use the app during active patrols.

60%
Less WhatsApp dependence
40%
Less search time
35%
More reports completed
25%
More active volunteers
Key screens
Tours search
Tour card
Milestone
Person profile
Health data
Design decisions
Home — stats as motivation
Stats visible on entry (amigos asistidos, recorridas) act as a built-in incentive. Volunteers see their cumulative impact before doing anything else.
Person profile — all info in one hub
Health, documents, emergency contacts, visits — all from one screen with icon grid. Critical during an active patrol where time and attention are limited.
Reports list — last editor visible
Shows who last edited each report — volunteers coordinate without duplicating updates, reducing friction between team members on patrol.
Reflection
What I learned

Designing for a nighttime, high-stress context forced me to question every decision through the lens of cognitive load. Roboto, large tap targets, and minimal navigation were functional requirements, not aesthetic choices.

What I'd do differently

I'd invest more time testing in a real nighttime scenario. Context changes everything: lighting, one-handed use, cold weather. Field testing would have shaped earlier decisions and probably changed some UI patterns.

Case study 02 · 2024 · Academic feature proposal

Wallapop

Sellers who needed to pause their activity had only one option: delete everything and lose their position. Many left instead.

Role
Feature Designer
Type
Feature design · Mobile
Methods
Benchmark · Wireframes · Risk mapping
Tools
Figma
Overview
Context

Wallapop is a circular economy marketplace where active listings drive engagement. The more sellers stay, the healthier the platform.

Problem

Sellers who needed to step away temporarily had only one option: delete their listings and lose their position entirely. Many chose to abandon the platform instead of starting over.

Hypothesis

If sellers could pause without losing their position, fewer would leave. The challenge was designing that feature without creating new problems — abuse, buyer confusion, or cluttered results.

Research findings
Real user reviewsPain point
José M. · Sep 2023
"I love the app but I had to delete some of my listings because I was going to be away. There should be a way to pause them temporarily."
Manuel S.S · Sep 2023
"It would be great to deactivate or suspend listings temporarily. If you go on holiday, you shouldn't have to delete everything."
Competitive benchmarkOpportunity
A
Airbnb
Allows "snooze" — listings preserved with no ranking penalty.
e
eBay
Vacation mode with a visible label on the seller profile.
V
Vinted
Profile visible with paused sales clearly labeled.
User flow — Modo Ausente
Complete task flow — 4 screens My profile → Calendar → Confirmation → Updated profile
Sellers select absence dates → listings paused automatically → profile shows unavailability → auto-reactivation on return with no ranking penalty.
Risk mitigation
Abuse prevention

A quarterly day limit prevents sellers from using away mode permanently or gaming the ranking system.

Buyer transparency

A clear badge on product cards and seller profile shows unavailability dates — buyers always know the situation.

Search control

An "Only available now" filter lets buyers exclude paused sellers when they need immediate purchases.

Case study 03 · 2024

Pharma Prospect

The company had evolved — but the website still reflected the old version. Visitors landed on something cold and out of step with who they'd become.

Role
UX Designer
Type
Web redesign
Methods
Sitemap · IA · Content strategy
User
Purchasing Manager, pharma (32–42yo)
Overview
Context

Pharma Prospect had evolved — their values, tone and focus had shifted. But the website still reflected the old version: industrial, cold, and disconnected from the company they had become.

Problem

Visitors landed on a page that felt out of step with the company's direction. The tone was cold, the hierarchy was unclear, and sustainability — a core value — was nearly invisible.

Solution

A full redesign to align the digital experience with the brand's new direction: more human, more transparent, more credible — from the first screen to the last.

New website — redesigned pages
Home
Human-first narrative from the first screen
The redesigned home opens with a corporate video and a sustainability-led headline — "Sumamos compromiso para un futuro más consciente." A green palette signals environmental commitment immediately. Sections now follow a progressive narrative: who we are → what we make → our values → contact.
Nosotros
From "Empresa" to "Nosotros" — people at the center
Renaming "Empresa" to "Nosotros" was a small but meaningful language decision. The page now showcases real people — workers, teams, facilities — building credibility and trust with pharmaceutical purchasing managers who evaluate suppliers on both quality and values.
Sostenibilidad
A new top-level section — not an afterthought
Sustainability was elevated from a buried mention to a primary navigation section. This makes it immediately visible to pharma purchasing managers who evaluate suppliers on ESG criteria — turning a differentiator into a front-page statement.

Case study 04 · UX QA Testing

Payment Flow Testing

The client needed confidence that their booking flow worked for real users, across two countries and two currencies. It didn't.

Role
UX QA Tester
Client
Premium retreat booking (EU + UK)
Rounds
3 iterative testing rounds
Tools
Awesome Screenshot · Sheets
The challenge
Scope

The client needed confidence that their booking flow worked — not in theory, but for real users in real conditions, across two countries and two currencies.

Technical focus

Did flows complete without errors? Were prices consistent between screens? Did the system behave correctly across all 4 transaction types?

UX focus

Was the pricing transparent and trustworthy? Were there moments of friction or confusion a real user would hit — even if the flow technically worked?

Process — 3 iterative rounds
Round 01
Detection
4 independent real transactions simulating a new user. Detected critical pricing inconsistencies in Greece — discrepancies of up to 32% between on-screen totals and actual card charges. Italy was exact from round 1.
Round 02
Verification post-fixes
After client corrections, re-tested all problematic flows. Verified improvements and documented remaining friction — including desktop navigation errors and deposit flow failures that persisted.
Round 03
Final validation
Confirmed complete financial accuracy across all tested scenarios. Full sign-off delivered with documentation covering both devices and both currencies.
Key findings
Critical — 32% pricing discrepancy: Difference between on-screen total and actual card charge in GBP flows (Greece). A financial trust failure for users completing a premium booking.
Critical — deposit flow crash: Site crashed with "critical error" on deposit flow in both countries. Reproduced consistently across multiple sessions.
Mobile — form load failure: Checkout form stopped loading after selecting country of origin. Blocked completion of the transaction entirely.
Desktop — unresponsive CTA: "Continue to payment" button did not respond. Users had to navigate via progress bar — a non-obvious workaround.
Italy — financially exact from round 1: Used as benchmark to isolate Greece-specific issues and validate what a correct flow should look like.
Outcome
Result

After 3 iterative rounds, the client achieved a financially precise and reliable system across all tested scenarios. Reports included visual evidence, reproduction steps, and actionable recommendations per issue.

What this shows

UX QA is not just bug-finding — it's advocating for the user at a technical level. A 32% pricing discrepancy isn't just a bug; it's a broken trust signal. Framing issues in terms of user impact makes reports actionable for both developers and product owners.

About

Research first.
Design second.

I'm a UX/UI designer based in Barcelona, focused on creating digital experiences that are clear, accessible, and grounded in real user needs.

My process always starts with understanding people — their context, their frustrations, and what would actually make their experience better. Then I translate that into design decisions I can justify.

I work best in projects where the problem is complex and the solution has to be genuinely useful, not just visually polished.
